symptoms.symptom_name: High-energy blunt chest trauma (3 m fall onto the side)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Blunt thoracic trauma is the commonest cause of a traumatic pneumothorax that can tension.
The same mechanism can lacerate intercostal/pulmonary vessels.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe air hunger, unable to speak in sentences

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Progressive lung collapse and mediastinal shift produce escalating respiratory distress.
Reflects critical hypoxaemia from a collapsed lung; a bystander-observable red flag.

symptoms.symptom_name: Deteriorating conscious state (agitation, confusion, drifting to unconsciousness)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cerebral hypoxia and falling cardiac output — a peri-arrest sign.
Pleuritic pain localises the injury to the right hemithorax.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: simple rib fractures with chest-wall pain
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rib fractures cause the same pleuritic pain — the red-herring that invites under-triage.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absent breath sounds over the right hemithorax

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Unilateral absent breath sounds with the right clinical context is a cardinal tension sign; sealed clinician auscultation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Hyperresonant percussion over the right hemithorax

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hyperresonance distinguishes trapped air from the dullness of a haemothorax.
A haemothorax gives a dull percussion note, not hyperresonance.

symptoms.symptom_name: Distended neck veins with hypotension (BP 75/40) and tachycardia (HR 138)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Obstructive shock signature — raised venous pressure with a falling blood pressure from mediastinal shift; sealed clinician measurement.
Massive blood loss typically gives flat, not distended, neck veins.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tracheal deviation to the left (away from the affected side)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A late sign of significant mediastinal shift; sealed clinician palpation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Subcutaneous emphysema over the right chest wall and neck

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Air tracking into the soft tissues confirms an air leak; sealed clinician palpation.
Objective critical hypoxia; scene measurement, not obtainable via chat (patient has no home device).

symptoms.symptom_name: Chest radiograph — right lung collapse with contralateral mediastinal shift

investigation_details.expected_result: Complete right lung collapse with mediastinal shift to the left
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Imaging must never delay decompression in a tension pneumothorax — it is confirmatory only and unavailable in telehealth.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Radiographic confirmation post-decompression; never a telehealth-available finding and never a reason to delay 000.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Absent breath sounds + hyperresonance + distended neck veins + hypotension after chest trauma is the classic bedside signature demanding immediate decompression.
The features available to the AI Doctor over telehealth — trauma mechanism, air hunger, cyanosis and falling consciousness — are alone sufficient to mandate 000.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency ambulance activation (000) and trauma-centre transport
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The only appropriate 'investigation/management' step from telehealth — definitive assessment and decompression are in-person.
Must not be sought before decompression in a suspected tension pneumothorax, and is unobtainable in telehealth; it is confirmatory only.
